Planning Guides 14 min read

Maryland Wedding Planning Checklist: Month-by-Month

Maryland wedding planning checklist for 2026. What to do 12, 9, 6, and 3 months before your wedding, with Maryland-specific venue and license timings.

J

John

Baltimore Wedding Planner

Featured image, replace with real photo

Maryland wedding planning has some specific timing realities that generic checklists miss. Popular Baltimore and Annapolis venues book 12 to 18 months in advance. The Naval Academy graduation weekend in late May fills the entire Annapolis hotel market. And the marriage license requires both of you to appear in person at a Circuit Court, which needs to be scheduled into the timeline.

This checklist is built around Maryland's actual constraints, not a generic national template. I've used versions of this with clients across 50+ Maryland weddings and refined it based on what consistently causes problems when it gets pushed back.

What Should You Do 12 to 18 Months Before Your Maryland Wedding?

The first decisions you make are the ones with the longest lead times. Get them locked in before anything else, and don't wait on the venue because you're not ready to commit. Venue availability determines your date more than your preferences will.

12-18 Months Out

  • Set your approximate guest count and total budget
  • Choose your wedding date range (3 to 4 potential dates)
  • Research and tour venues, book as soon as you find the right one
  • Book your photographer (they book even faster than venues)
  • Hire a wedding planner or coordinator if using one
  • Start a broad vendor research list
  • If ceremony is in a Maryland state or county park: start permit research now
  • Block hotel rooms for out-of-town guests (especially for Annapolis or Eastern Shore)
  • Have the budget conversation with both families if relevant

Maryland-specific note: If you're considering late May, confirm your date doesn't conflict with Naval Academy graduation weekend, which fills all Annapolis accommodations within 30 miles.

What Should You Do 9 to 11 Months Before Your Maryland Wedding?

Venue's locked. Now book the vendors who fill up next fastest. If you're getting married in peak season, don't assume you've got time here.

9-11 Months Out

  • Book your caterer or confirm venue catering details
  • Book your DJ or band
  • Start dress shopping (most dresses take 4 to 6 months to arrive)
  • Book your officiant or confirm your friend's ordination plan
  • Create your preliminary guest list
  • Research florists and schedule consultations
  • Book engagement photo session with your photographer
  • Research rehearsal dinner venues if needed
  • Read all vendor contracts before signing, watch for catering exclusivity clauses

What Should You Do 6 to 8 Months Before Your Maryland Wedding?

The planning picks up pace here. Most couples hit their first real stress period in this window when the guest list and invitation decisions can't be pushed back anymore. They've got to become real decisions with real numbers. If you've been avoiding the guest list conversation, now's when it has to happen.

6-8 Months Out

  • Finalize your guest list (both of you must agree before invitations are designed)
  • Design and order invitations (allow 4 to 6 weeks for printing)
  • Book hair and makeup artists
  • Book videographer if using one
  • Book florist
  • Book wedding cake or desserts
  • Arrange transportation (guest shuttles, getaway car)
  • Set up a wedding website with travel and accommodation info for guests
  • Create or register on a gift registry
  • Book and confirm rehearsal dinner venue

What Should You Do 3 to 5 Months Before Your Maryland Wedding?

3-5 Months Out

  • Mail invitations (8 to 10 weeks before the wedding)
  • Schedule dress fittings
  • Write or finalize your vows
  • Book honeymoon travel if applicable
  • Create a detailed day-of timeline draft
  • Confirm all vendor bookings in writing
  • Obtain your Maryland marriage license (can be done as early as 6 months out)
  • Confirm accommodations for yourselves and wedding party
  • Assign wedding party roles and responsibilities

Maryland-specific note: Your marriage license is valid for 6 months and can be used the same day you receive it in most counties. Apply at your chosen Circuit Court with both of you present, valid photo ID, and Social Security numbers. See the full Maryland marriage license guide.

What Should You Do 1 to 2 Months Before Your Maryland Wedding?

1-2 Months Out

  • Finalize your seating chart as RSVPs come in
  • Give final guest count to caterer
  • Final dress fitting
  • Confirm all vendor arrival times
  • Prepare vendor payments and tip envelopes
  • Send final timeline to all vendors
  • Pack for honeymoon if traveling immediately after
  • Create a day-of emergency kit list
  • Share final guest count and layout with venue coordinator
  • Confirm rehearsal dinner details

What Should You Do the Week Before Your Maryland Wedding?

The Final Week

  • Attend and run the ceremony rehearsal
  • Confirm all vendor arrival times one final time
  • Give wedding party members their specific timing and instructions
  • Confirm your marriage license is signed and ready (officiant brings it to the ceremony)
  • Hand off vendor payments and tips to your coordinator or a trusted person
  • Get genuinely good sleep the night before (everything's in order by now)
  • After the ceremony: make sure the signed marriage license is collected and will be filed within 60 days

What Are the Maryland-Specific Planning Timelines to Know?

Generic checklists don't account for these. If you're planning a Maryland wedding and you've been using a national template, these are the gaps you'll want to close before they become problems.

Task When to do it Maryland-specific note
Venue booking12 to 18 months outBaltimore and Annapolis peak season Saturdays book this far out
Marriage license1 day to 6 months beforeBoth must appear in person at a Circuit Court. License valid 6 months.
Park/state park permit2 to 6 months outMaryland DNR permits available up to 6 months in advance. Peak dates fill.
Hotel room block9 to 12 months outEastern Shore and Annapolis accommodations fill fast in peak season
Dress order9 to 10 months out4 to 6 months production time + 2 months for alterations
Naval Academy datesBefore setting any dateLate May graduation fills all Annapolis-area hotels within 30 miles
License filingWithin 60 days of ceremonyOfficiant signs at ceremony. Signed license must be returned to issuing Circuit Court.

Frequently Asked Questions About Maryland Wedding Planning Timelines

What's the most common mistake couples make in Maryland wedding planning?

Waiting too long on the venue. Maryland's most popular venues (Evergreen Museum, Clipper Mill, Quiet Waters Park) book 12 to 18 months in advance for peak season Saturdays. Couples who start planning in January for an October wedding often find their preferred venue is already gone. Book the venue before you book anything else, and treat the date as flexible until you find a venue with availability.

When should you hire a wedding planner in Maryland?

For full-service planning, hire your planner as soon as possible after getting engaged, before you've booked any vendors. The earlier you start, the more venue and vendor options you'll have. For partial planning, the most useful entry point is around 4 to 6 months before the wedding when vendor sourcing and contract review are still actionable. For day-of coordination, 3 to 4 months minimum. See all package options.

Can you plan a Maryland wedding in less than 6 months?

Yes, but your venue options will be limited to what's available at short notice, and you'll need to work quickly on vendor bookings. The marriage license can be obtained the same day you need it in most Maryland counties. Short-timeline weddings work best with off-peak dates (November through March), weekday ceremonies, or smaller guest counts that give you more venue flexibility. A partial planning package focused on the final stretch is particularly useful for couples in this situation.

Do you need a wedding planner to use this checklist?

No. This checklist works for couples planning on their own. A planner helps you move through it faster and with fewer mistakes, but the timeline itself applies regardless of whether you're DIYing it or working with a professional. The main benefit a planner provides is vendor vetting, contract review, and someone managing the logistics on the actual day so you don't have to.

John uses a refined version of this checklist with every Maryland client. If you want to talk through where you are in the planning process and what's realistic for your timeline, the free consultation is a good starting point.
